replies.text: ​​​@Gorillaslips Yeah, no. You do not use them in those circumstances, either. It's DVDs, "p"s and "q"s, and ifs. Funny how every example you gave was also incorrect. Just because far too many "professionals" misuse apostrophes doesn't make their misuse suddenly correct. It's like no matter how many people misuse "literally," it will never mean "figuratively," and "ouija" will never be correctly pronounced as "wee-gee," regardless of how many numpties say it wrong (it's "wee-ja," btw, the instructions tell you this).

replies.author: @Gorillaslips
replies.text:
@englishatheart Ah, the sacred laws of apostrophes — if only all style guides agreed as confidently as you do. You’re right that DVDs is the modern standard, but the others aren’t grammatical heresy.

The Chicago Manual of Style (7.15), Oxford English Dictionary, and Fowler’s Modern English Usage all allow apostrophes in plurals of single letters or words treated as words — hence Mind your p’s and q’s and too many “if’s.” It’s a clarity thing, not a grammar crime.

So while DVDs may be “pure,” p’s and q’s and if’s are both respectable old-timers in good standing. (And if we’re consulting spirits about ouija, I’ll trust the ghosts over the gatekeepers.)

It's funny how much a simple web-search could have done for you.

Speak what you truly believe if you must speak on such matters, not what is merely fashionable, because fashions change with the times. And I think Emma, like some others, is learning this lesson too late.
